title: Composing pages from plain HTML
excerpt: Header and footer live in their own .html files and get pulled in by a tiny custom element — write once, share everywhere.
content:
The header and footer you see on every page aren't copied into each page. They live once, in partials/header.html and partials/footer.html, and get pulled in by a tiny custom element:

<html-include src='partials/header.html'></html-include>

The element is about twenty lines. It fetches its src, drops the markup into itself, and announces when it's done:

class HtmlInclude extends HTMLElement {
  connectedCallback() {
    const url = new URL(this.getAttribute('src'), document.baseURI);
    fetch(url)
      .then(r => r.text())
      .then(html => { this.innerHTML = html; });
  }
}
customElements.define('html-include', HtmlInclude);

Why light DOM

The fragment is injected as ordinary page content, not into a shadow root, so the site's global stylesheet styles it like everything else. No per-component CSS plumbing required.

Two things to know

  • Resolve against document.baseURI. That makes the same paths work whether the site is served from a domain root or a project subpath like /repo/.
  • Scripts inside an included fragment don't run. Markup set via innerHTML never executes its <script> tags — so anything interactive (like the theme toggle) is its own custom element, registered up front and upgraded automatically wherever it appears.

The page bodies work the same way: the router fetches pages/*.html fragments and composes them into <main>. Header, footer, and every page — all just files.

title: Page transitions, no library
excerpt: The View Transitions API animates between two DOM states from a single function call — and morphs a blog card into its article.
content:
Clicking between pages here doesn't cut — it cross-fades, and a post's title glides out of the list and into the article. That's the View Transitions API, and it's built into the browser. No animation library, no bundle.

The idea is almost too simple: you hand the browser a function that changes the DOM. It snapshots the page before, runs your function, snapshots after, and animates the difference.

function update() {
  main.innerHTML = nextPageHTML; // change the DOM however you like
}

if (document.startViewTransition && !prefersReducedMotion) {
  document.startViewTransition(update); // animated
} else {
  update(); // instant fallback
}

Morphing one element into another

To make the post title travel between the list and the article, both elements get the same view-transition-name:

<!-- in the list -->
<h3 style='view-transition-name: post-json-as-a-module'>…</h3>

<!-- in the article -->
<h1 style='view-transition-name: post-json-as-a-module'>…</h1>

The browser sees the matching name across the two snapshots and interpolates position, size, and opacity between them. Everything without a name simply cross-fades.

Same-document, on purpose

There are two flavours: same-document (for single-page apps) and cross-document (for plain multi-page sites). Same-document transitions are Baseline in Chrome 111+, Safari 18+, and Firefox 144+. Cross-document transitions don't work in Firefox yet — which is exactly why this site keeps everything in one document and routes with the hash.

One rule worth keeping: wrap the effect in a check for prefers-reduced-motion and skip straight to the DOM update when someone asks for less motion.

title: The blog is one JSON file
excerpt: Import attributes let you load JSON straight into a module — no fetch(), no JSON.parse(), no bundler.
content:
This entire blog — every post you're reading — lives in a single file, data/posts.json. There's no database and no CMS. A JavaScript module imports the file directly:

import posts from '../data/posts.json' with { type: 'json' };

// posts is already a parsed array — no fetch(), no JSON.parse()
const latest = [...posts].sort((a, b) => new Date(b.date) - new Date(a.date));

That with { type: 'json' } is an import attribute. It tells the engine the file must be parsed as JSON, and — just as importantly — it's a security check: the browser refuses the import unless the server sends the file as application/json. A file extension alone isn't trusted.

What it needs

  • The file must be served as application/json. GitHub Pages does this for .json automatically.
  • It only works over HTTP(S). Opening the page from file:// won't load modules at all, so use a local server while developing.
  • JSON modules expose a default export only — there are no named exports.

Support

JSON module imports are Baseline across current browsers: Chrome and Edge 123+, Safari 17.2+, and Firefox 128+. The older assert { type: 'json' } spelling is deprecated — always use with.

Both the list and the article view import the same file. The browser fetches it once and reuses it, so adding a post is as simple as editing one JSON file and pushing.

title: Routing without a server
excerpt: GitHub Pages only serves files. Here's how to get real navigation anyway — using the one part of the URL the server never sees.
content:
A static host like GitHub Pages does exactly one thing: hand back a file that already exists at a path. Ask for /blog and, unless there's a blog.html sitting there, you get a 404. That's the whole problem with client-side routing on a static host — refresh a deep link and the server looks for a file that was never there.

The fix is the one part of the URL the server never sees: the fragment after the #. Anything past the hash stays in the browser. The server only ever receives a request for the root, so it always finds index.html and the page boots from there.

One entry, many views

Every address on this site is really index.html plus a hash — #/, #/about, #/blog/json-as-a-module. A small router reads the hash, matches it against a table of routes, and swaps the contents of <main>. No file per page on disk; no path the host can't serve.

function parseHash() {
  const h = location.hash;
  if (h === '' || h === '#' || h === '#/') return '/';
  if (h.startsWith('#/')) return h.slice(1); // '#/about' -> '/about'
  return null; // e.g. '#main' — an in-page anchor, not a route
}

addEventListener('hashchange', render);

Because navigation is just the hash changing, plain <a href='#/about'> links work with no click handlers at all — the browser fires hashchange and the router does the rest.

The one edge case

If someone types a real path like /blog directly, the host still 404s before any JavaScript runs. A tiny 404.html catches that, turns the path into a hash, and redirects to the root — so even hand-typed deep links land in the right place.
